journal_entry.description: Board declared cash dividend payable in 30 days
journal_entry.rationale: When a dividend is declared, the company creates a legal obligation to pay shareholders. Under IFRS, this creates a liability (Dividends Payable) at declaration date. The debit goes to Dividends (or Retained Earnings) which reduces equity. The dividend account increases with a debit (slot b=1), and liability increases with a credit (slot a=1). No tax applies to dividend distributions. The dividend will be paid in 30 days, at which point Dividends Payable will be debited and Cash credited.

output.reason: Declaration of cash dividend debits Dividends (owner distribution account), which is a dividend increase. This represents a distribution to shareholders and is not an expense. The credit side (not classified here) would be to Dividends Payable liability.

debit_classifier.reason: Declaration of cash dividend debits Dividends (owner distribution account), which is a dividend increase. This represents a distribution to shareholders and is not an expense. The credit side (not classified here) would be to Dividends Payable liability.

output.reason: When a cash dividend is declared, the company records: (1) a debit to Dividends (or Retained Earnings), and (2) a credit to Dividends Payable (a liability). The credit side shows a liability increase because the company now owes the dividend payment to shareholders. This is recorded even though payment will occur in 30 days.

output.reason: Entry correctly records dividend declaration. Debit to Dividends ($500,000) reduces equity, credit to Dividends Payable ($500,000) creates liability. Balance verified (debits = credits). Under IFRS, dividend creates legal obligation at declaration date. Amount matches transaction text. No tax applies to dividend distributions. Directionality correct: dividend account increases with debit (equity reduction), liability increases with credit.
